name: TRON USDT stablecoin fraud flow studies
authors_or_org: John M. Griffin & Kevin Mei (UT Austin, SSRN 2024/rev.2025); Luciano Juvinski, Haochen Li, Alessio Brini (StableAML, arXiv 2602.17842, 2026); Tether / TRON / TRM Labs (T3 Financial Crime Unit); BlockSec / crypto.news (USDT blacklisting analytics)
core_method: A cluster of empirical / forensic-finance and applied-AML work characterizing illicit stablecoin flows (predominantly USDT on TRON and Ethereum). (1) Griffin & Mei, 'How Do Crypto Flows Finance Slavery? The Economics of Pig Butchering' (SSRN 4742235): forensic on-chain tracing — collected crypto addresses from 4,000+ pig-butchering victims and traced fund flows from victims through scammer wallets to exchange deposit accounts, quantifying volumes, fees, and the central role of Tether. (2) StableAML (arXiv 2602.17842): builds a large stablecoin-specific labeled dataset (USDT+USDC full transfer history on Ethereum) from Etherscan reports, security firms and the OFAC SDN list, with 68 domain-specific behavioral wallet features across four categories (Interaction, Derived Network, Transfer-based, Temporal/Direct), and benchmarks tree-ensemble models vs GNNs for wallet-level illicit detection. (3) T3 Financial Crime Unit: operational public-private tracing/freezing pipeline combining TRM Labs analytics with Tether's USDT freeze authority on TRON. (4) USDT blacklisting practice studies (BlockSec/crypto.news): empirical measurement of Tether's on-chain blacklist (freeze) events as ground-truth illicit labels.
graph_task_type: Mixed: address/wallet-level node classification (StableAML behavioral wallet detection); flow-tracing / path analysis rather than formal graph-ML in Griffin & Mei and the T3/blacklisting work
temporal_modeling: Griffin & Mei track flows over calendar time (2020-2024) to estimate annual volumes and observe the growth of pig-butchering deposits. StableAML includes explicit Temporal and Direct features (velocity, timing of transfers) and finds cybercrime-syndicate wallets exhibit high-velocity dispersion vs the static footprints of sanctioned entities. Blacklisting studies are time-stamped freeze-event series (e.g. $515M frozen across 371 wallets in a 30-day window). No continuous-time GNN modeling.
class_imbalance_handling: StableAML addresses imbalance implicitly via domain-informed tree ensembles evaluated on Macro-F1 (robust to skew) and by curating a focused illicit-wallet set rather than sampling the whole chain. Griffin & Mei and the blacklisting studies are descriptive/empirical and do not apply ML imbalance techniques. [uncertain]
heterophily_handling: Not formally modeled as graph heterophily. StableAML notes GNNs struggle with the increasing FRAGMENTATION of stablecoin transaction networks (laundering deliberately breaks graph connectivity), which is why domain behavioral features on tree ensembles outperform GNNs — an empirical argument that graph-structure/heterophily assumptions weaken for stablecoin laundering. [uncertain]
supervision_regime: StableAML: supervised (labels from Etherscan/security-firm reports + OFAC SDN). Griffin & Mei: unsupervised/forensic tracing with victim-sourced seed labels. T3/blacklisting: label-generating (produces ground-truth illicit addresses via freezes).
edge_and_direction_support: Flow direction (victim -> scammer -> exchange) is central to Griffin & Mei's tracing and to StableAML's Transfer-based and Derived Network features (in/out amounts, counterparties). Amounts and timing are exploited as features; formal directed-multigraph GNN edge modeling is not the focus (StableAML explicitly finds feature-based models beat GNNs here).
typology_coverage: Strong ground-truth coverage of real-world laundering/fraud typologies on TRON/USDT: pig-butchering (romance/investment scams financing forced-labor 'scam compounds' in SE Asia), gather-scatter / dispersion patterns (StableAML high-velocity syndicate flows), sanctions-evasion and terrorism financing (OFAC SDN wallets, ISIS-K-linked TRON wallets, North Korea-linked flows), and exchange cash-out (funds consolidated into suspicious exchange deposit accounts). This is the empirical typology ground-truth the ML methods lack.
chain_coverage: TRON (TRC-20 USDT is the dominant illicit rail; ~$506M of 2025 freezes on TRON) and Ethereum (StableAML is Ethereum USDT/USDC; ~$8.73M of 2025 freezes on ETH). Effectively multi-chain USDT-centric.
datasets_used: Griffin & Mei: proprietary set of crypto addresses from 4,000+ pig-butchering victims + on-chain flow tracing (2020-2024). StableAML: custom large-scale dataset = full USDT+USDC transfer history on Ethereum labeled from Etherscan illicit reports, blockchain security firms, and OFAC SDN list (68 features). T3/BlockSec: Tether on-chain blacklist (freeze) events as a public ground-truth label source (4,163 addresses / $1.26B in 2025). No Elliptic/Elliptic++/AMLworld.
key_metrics: Griffin & Mei: ~$27.8B/yr moved into suspicious exchange deposit accounts (2021-2023) at ~33 bps fees; >$75B moved to exchanges over Jan2020-Feb2024; ~84% of scam transaction volume converted to Tether. USDT blacklisting 2025: 4,163 unique addresses blacklisted, $1.26B frozen (~$3.4M/day), $698M permanently burned, only 3.6% unfrozen; TRON $506M vs Ethereum $8.73M; 2023-2025 cumulative $3.29B across 7,268 addresses. T3 FCU: >$300M frozen by Oct 2025 (>$450M by 2026), $100M by Jan 2025, $250M by Aug 2025. StableAML: domain tree ensembles achieve higher Macro-F1 than GNNs (exact figures in paper). [uncertain]
explainability_approach: StableAML emphasizes interpretability: its domain-feature tree ensembles let analysts read WHY a wallet is flagged and successfully DIFFERENTIATE high-velocity cybercrime-syndicate dispersion from the static footprints of OFAC-sanctioned entities. Griffin & Mei is inherently narrative/evidentiary (documented fund-flow paths from victims to exchanges). Blacklisting/T3 work provides authoritative binary ground truth with case-level narratives (FBI/Europol requests, sanctions matches).
explainability_type: StableAML: feature-attribution on tree ensembles (SHAP/feature-importance style, rule-interpretable). Griffin & Mei & T3: rule-based / narrative forensic evidence.
code_availability: StableAML: arXiv preprint with HTML/PDF; code release not confirmed. [uncertain] Griffin & Mei: working paper, proprietary victim data not released. T3/blacklisting: operational, not open-source; BlockSec provides a public USDT Freeze & Blacklist Checker tool.
data_availability: USDT blacklist/freeze events are fully public on-chain and queryable (BlockSec USDT Freeze Checker; Tether contract blacklist calls) — a strong free ground-truth label source. Griffin & Mei victim-address set is proprietary. StableAML dataset is derived from public on-chain USDT/USDC + public OFAC SDN + Etherscan reports (reconstructable); explicit public download not confirmed. [uncertain]
scalability: T3/blacklisting operates at full-chain production scale in real time (continuous freezes across TRON+Ethereum, dozens of jurisdictions). Griffin & Mei traced billions in flows offline. StableAML processes the full USDT/USDC transfer history on Ethereum, feasible with tree ensembles on CPU (a deliberate advantage over GNNs which the authors report do not scale well to fragmented stablecoin graphs).
deployment_maturity: T3 Financial Crime Unit and Tether USDT blacklisting = production systems (live asset freezing). Griffin & Mei = published empirical study. StableAML = academic prototype (2026 preprint).
integration_difficulty: Low-to-medium for ChainLens. USDT freeze/blacklist events are free, public, and directly usable as ground-truth positive labels for TRON TRC-20 address scoring (solves the labeling bottleneck). Griffin & Mei's typologies (victim->scammer->exchange gather-scatter) give ready rule-motif templates. StableAML's 68 behavioral features are straightforward to re-engineer on TRC-20 transfer data. Main effort is TRON data ingestion and mapping typologies to motif rules.
relevance_to_chainlens: Directly foundational for ChainLens's TRON TRC-20 scoring. (1) LABELS: Tether's on-chain USDT blacklist (4,163 addresses/$1.26B in 2025, ~$506M on TRON) is a free, high-precision ground-truth positive set ChainLens can scrape to train/evaluate TRC-20 address scoring — filling the gap left by Ethereum-only academic datasets. (2) FEATURES: StableAML's four feature families (Interaction, Derived Network, Transfer-based, Temporal/Direct) and its finding that domain behavioral features on tree ensembles beat GNNs on FRAGMENTED stablecoin graphs is a critical design signal — ChainLens should pair its GraphSAGE/GCN with strong SNA/behavioral features and not assume graph structure alone suffices for USDT laundering. (3) TYPOLOGIES & NARRATIVE: Griffin & Mei's documented pig-butchering flow (victim -> scammer -> Tether conversion -> suspicious exchange deposit; gather-scatter, 33bps fees) gives concrete rule-motifs and evidence-narrative templates for ChainLens's output, and the T3 case narratives (sanctions, ISIS-K/DPRK links) enrich the typology taxonomy. (4) VALIDATION: freeze/burn outcomes provide an external oracle to validate ChainLens risk scores.
key_takeaways: Tether's public USDT blacklist (4,163 addresses / $1.26B frozen in 2025, ~$506M on TRON, $698M burned) is a free, high-quality ground-truth positive-label source ChainLens should scrape for TRON TRC-20 address scoring — solves the biggest labeling gap.

StableAML's headline lesson: on fragmented stablecoin laundering graphs, domain behavioral features + tree ensembles OUTPERFORM GNNs on Macro-F1 — ChainLens should invest heavily in SNA/behavioral features and treat GNNs as a complement, not a replacement.

Griffin & Mei quantify the pig-butchering economy (~$27.8B/yr into suspicious exchange deposits, ~84% converted to Tether, 33bps fees) and hand ChainLens ready-made typology motifs: victim->scammer->USDT->exchange gather-scatter cash-out.
The illicit rail is overwhelmingly USDT on TRON (TRON $506M vs Ethereum $8.73M of 2025 freezes) — validates ChainLens's TRC-20 focus and argues for prioritizing TRON ingestion.

Freeze/burn outcomes (only 3.6% unfrozen; $698M permanently destroyed) plus T3/OFAC case narratives give ChainLens both an external validation oracle for risk scores and a richer typology taxonomy (sanctions evasion, terrorism financing, DPRK/ISIS-K links).
